title: Enterprise Account Executive
setting: A large technology company selling enterprise software to major corporations
description: You're an Enterprise Account Executive selling complex, high-value solutions to Australia's largest organisations — banks, telcos, government agencies, and ASX-listed companies. Your deals are six to seven figures, your sales cycles are 6-18 months, and you're managing multiple stakeholders across procurement, IT, business units, and the C-suite. This is strategic, relationship-driven selling at the highest level.
salary: $150,000-$300,000 AUD OTE. Base salary typically $120,000-$180,000 with $30,000-$120,000 in variable commission. Accelerators can push total comp above $400,000 for exceptional years. Some roles include equity/RSUs.

schedule.activity: Strategic reading: review industry news, ASX announcements, and LinkedIn activity for your top 10 accounts
schedule.note: Enterprise selling is about knowing your customer's world better than they do

schedule.activity: Account strategy: review your account planning canvas for today's key meetings. Update stakeholder notes.
schedule.note: Before any enterprise meeting, know every attendee's priorities and concerns

schedule.activity: Internal alignment: call with your Solutions Engineer to prep for a technical deep-dive with the CTO of a major bank
schedule.note: Enterprise deals are team sports. Your SE, Customer Success Manager, and executive sponsor all play roles.

schedule.activity: Executive meeting: QBR presentation to the CIO and VP Technology at a top-3 account ($500K ACV renewal + $200K expansion)
schedule.note: This is not a product review — it's a strategic business review. Lead with their metrics, not yours.

schedule.activity: Debrief with your account team: what went well, what concerns were raised, action items for each person
schedule.note: Document everything. Enterprise deals have long memories — something mentioned today will matter in 6 months.

schedule.activity: RFP response: working with the bid team to craft a response to a government agency RFP ($1.2M deal)
schedule.note: RFP responses are a team effort. Your job is to ensure the response tells a story, not just answers questions.

schedule.activity: Executive lunch: meeting a CTO at a prospective account for an informal relationship-building lunch
schedule.note: Enterprise relationships are built over years, not calls. Face-to-face matters.

schedule.activity: Champion coaching: 1:1 call with your champion at a major deal to prep them for their internal business case presentation
schedule.note: The most important conversations happen when you're not in the room. Equip your champion to win internally.

schedule.activity: Procurement negotiation: call with procurement team at a large retailer. Discussing payment terms, SLAs, and data sovereignty.
schedule.note: Enterprise procurement teams are professional negotiators. Know your limits and trade strategically.

schedule.activity: Pipeline planning: review your $3M annual quota. Assess coverage, identify gaps, plan prospecting into new departments at existing accounts.
schedule.note: Enterprise AEs need 2-3x pipeline coverage. With 12-month cycles, next year's pipeline starts now.

schedule.activity: Executive alignment: brief your VP of Sales on the QBR outcomes and the government RFP strategy
schedule.note: Managing up is crucial. Your VP needs to know where to deploy executive-to-executive support.

schedule.activity: Networking event: industry event in the city — building relationships with enterprise buyers and partners
schedule.note: Enterprise selling extends beyond business hours. The relationships you build at events pay dividends.

responsibilities: Own a portfolio of 5-15 named enterprise accounts with $1M-$5M+ annual revenue potential
Build and execute strategic account plans with 90-day action milestones
Develop multi-threaded relationships across C-suite, IT, procurement, and business units
Navigate complex 6-18 month sales cycles with multiple evaluation phases
Lead cross-functional account teams: Solutions Engineers, Customer Success, Professional Services
Respond to RFPs and manage formal procurement processes
Deliver executive-level QBRs that drive expansion and protect renewals
Maintain detailed account intelligence and stakeholder maps
Forecast accurately — enterprise forecasting tolerates less variance than mid-market
challenges: Extremely long sales cycles — a deal you start today might not close for 12-18 months
Navigating organisational politics: multiple stakeholders with competing agendas
Procurement teams who are trained negotiators with leverage
Maintaining deal momentum when buying committees take weeks between meetings
Security, compliance, and legal reviews that can stall deals for months
The pressure of carrying a $2M-$5M quota on a small number of large, complex deals
Competitors with existing relationships and entrenched positions
rewards: Highest earning potential in sales — $200K-$400K+ AUD with accelerators
Working with Australia's most prestigious organisations and senior executives
Deeply strategic work — every deal is a complex puzzle requiring creativity and patience
Building career-long executive relationships that open doors for decades
Intellectual challenge: no two enterprise deals are alike
The satisfaction of closing a seven-figure deal you've worked on for a year
Clear path to executive leadership: CRO, VP Sales, Managing Director
careerPath: SDR/BDR at a tech company (0-1 year) — learn prospecting and qualification fundamentals
Mid-Market Account Executive (1-3 years) — develop full-cycle selling skills
Enterprise Account Executive (3-7 years) — complex, high-value deal management
Senior Enterprise AE / Strategic Account Director (7-10 years) — top-tier accounts, $5M+ portfolios
Regional Sales Director (10-15 years) — lead a team of enterprise AEs across a region
VP of Sales / CRO (15+ years) — own the entire go-to-market strategy and execution
Board Advisory / Consulting (20+ years) — leverage enterprise relationships and expertise
